omission_flags: The analysis omits the crucial 6 February 2026 Federal Court ruling that reinstated the government's broad powers under CMA s.233. This omission fundamentally alters the premise of the analysis, which falsely assumes the law is still restricted.
evasion_flags: By relying on a stale Court of Appeal ruling, the analysis evades addressing the reality that the government has already successfully regained and exercises the full sweeping powers of the CMA s.233 that Zahid is vaguely asking to be 'strengthened'.

cardinal_sin_flags.quote: Federal Court granted the government leave to appeal on 13 November 2025; the hearing has not been held. The main online-insult statute sits in legal limbo.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This is a stale, factually incorrect claim. The Federal Court heard the appeal and delivered its ruling on 6 February 2026, completely overturning the Court of Appeal and reinstating the 'offensive' and 'annoy' provisions. The statute is not in limbo.
cardinal_sin_flags.fix: State that the Federal Court overturned the Court of Appeal's decision on 6 February 2026, fully restoring the scope of CMA s.233.

cardinal_sin_flags.quote: Like calling for more speed cameras on a road where tickets just hit a five-year high — while appeal judges struck down the speed-limit signs themselves.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The analogy is fatally undermined by the stale fact. Because the apex court reinstated the law in Feb 2026, the 'speed-limit signs' are no longer struck down. This framing misleads the reader about the current state of the law.
cardinal_sin_flags.fix: Revise the analogy to reflect that the apex court has already reinstated the 'speed-limit signs,' meaning Zahid is calling for more power even after the government just won back its broadest enforcement tool.

verification_log.claim: On 10 May 2026 at the Musyawarah Nasional forum, DPM Ahmad Zahid called for 'firm, fair, wise' action against insults to Islam and the Malay Rulers, and a strengthened legal framework.
verification_log.primary_source: Bernama, 10 May 2026: 'Insults Against Islam, Malay Rulers Must Be Handled Firmly - Ahmad Zahid'

verification_log.claim: SUARAM said Sedition Act charges hit a five-year high in 2025.
verification_log.primary_source: Civicus Monitor, 16 July 2025, citing SUARAM Malaysia Human Rights Report 2025 Overview

verification_log.claim: CIJ counted 233 expression-law cases in 2025 — up 23% on 2024. CIJ's 2024 base was 189 cases.
verification_log.primary_source: Free Malaysia Today, 10 Dec 2025: 'Sedition Act cases up 84% in 2025, says CIJ'

verification_log.claim: On 19 August 2025 the Court of Appeal struck 'offensive' and 'annoy' from CMA s.233 — unconstitutional under Articles 8 and 10.
verification_log.primary_source: eLaw.my / FMT coverage of Heidy Quah Court of Appeal ruling 19 Aug 2025

verification_log.claim: Federal Court granted the government leave to appeal on 13 November 2025
the hearing has not been held. The main online-insult statute sits in legal limbo.
verification_log.primary_source: ARTICLE 19, 26 Feb 2026; Skrine Legal Alert March 2026
verification_log.corrected_value: The Federal Court heard the appeal and delivered its ruling on 6 February 2026, reinstating the words 'offensive' and 'annoy' in CMA s.233.

verification_log.claim: The bill meant to replace these statutes was promised in 2012. Fourteen years later, it is still in cabinet draft.
verification_log.primary_source: Global Voices/Bernama, 18 July 2012; The Star, 7 April 2026: 'Putrajaya to revisit proposed national harmony bill, says Aaron'

overall_assessment: The analysis successfully avoids racial and religious bias while tracking structural legal mechanics, but it commits a severe Cardinal Sin of relying on a stale, unverified detail. By missing the February 2026 Federal Court ruling that reinstated CMA s.233, it presents a false legal reality, incorrectly claiming the state's main online-insult tool is in 'limbo.' This error cascades, breaking the central analogy and misleading the reader about the government's current enforcement powers. While the civil society data is accurately verified, the legal foundation of the brief must be entirely corrected before publication.
